sources.notes: Read the full statement and constraints, including arbitrary grouping and leaving m mod k problems unused.
Official editorial confirms the solver-count reduction, sorted-block greedy, two-pointer or binary-search preprocessing, and harmonic bound.
Actually read; cross-checks the three difficulty cases, lower_bound solver counts, and every-k-th sorted contribution.
Actually read; independently states that the largest m mod k costs are discarded and the k-th, 2k-th, ... costs are the optimal maxima.
Proved the order-statistic lower bound and exhaustively matched the formula against optimal grouping on 1,848 small cost-multiset cases.

annotation.summary: For every contest size k, choose floor(m/k) disjoint k-problem contests to minimize the sum of Kevin's ranks.
annotation.constraints: Up to 5e4 test cases; 1 <= n,m <= 3e5 per case; sum n and sum m are each at most 3e5; ratings and difficulties are in [0,1e9]; answers are required for every k from 1 to m.
annotation.core_idea: Discard contestants with rating below a1, since they can never outrank Kevin. Give each problem a cost c: c=0 if Kevin solves it or nobody solves it; otherwise c is the number of contestants with rating at least its difficulty. In any contest Kevin's rank is 1+max(c), because among the problems he cannot solve, the easiest one determines exactly the nested suffix of contestants who solve something extra. For fixed k, let q=floor(m/k). Sort all costs, keep the qk smallest (equivalently discard the largest m mod k), and split them into consecutive blocks of k. The j-th smallest group maximum must be at least the (jk)-th smallest kept cost, and consecutive blocks attain all these bounds, so the answer is q plus the costs at sorted positions k,2k,...,qk. Sort ratings and difficulties and sweep them to obtain all c values; the total number of sampled positions over every k is sum floor(m/k)=O(m log m).
annotation.complexity: O(n log n + m log m) time and O(n+m) memory per test case; the all-k scan is bounded by the harmonic series.
tricks: Remove contestants who can never beat Kevin.
Map both Kevin-solvable and globally impossible problems to zero rank contribution.
Reduce each contest to the maximum solver-count cost of one problem.
Discard the largest remainder and group consecutive sorted costs.
Use the harmonic-series bound for all k.

solution_variants.name: solver-count-block-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Sort ratings and difficulties, use a monotone sweep to compute each problem's extra-solver count, sort those costs, and for every k sum one plus each consecutive block maximum at positions k,2k,... after omitting the largest remainder. Per-problem lower_bound is an interchangeable preprocessing implementation, not a materially different solution.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n + m log m) time and O(n+m) memory.

tags.evidence: After the rank reduction, sorting the costs exposes both the optimal discarded suffix and the exact k-th order statistics that must be group maxima.
For each k, greedily keep the qk smallest costs and group consecutive sorted costs; this simultaneously attains the lower bound on every ordered group maximum.
A selected cost can be exchanged with any smaller discarded cost, and interleaved groups can be normalized into consecutive sorted blocks without increasing their maxima.
With contestants and problems sorted by value, one monotone rating pointer gives the number of contestants able to solve each problem.
Computing every answer visits floor(m/k) block maxima for each k, totaling O(m log m) visits.
